questions.question: Explain the difference between power and energy in the context of mobile application energy consumption analysis.
keyPoints: Power is the rate of energy consumption (measured in Watts)
Energy is the total consumption over time (measured in Watt-hours or Joules)
Power = Energy/Time relationship
Practical example: 2W power consumption for 5 hours = 10Wh energy

questions.question: List and briefly explain three major sources of energy drain in mobile applications.
keyPoints: Screen display - LCD/OLED backlight consumes significant power
CPU processing - active computation uses high power
Network operations - cellular radio uses substantial power especially with weak signal
GPS/Location services - continuous location tracking drains battery
Wakelocks - prevent device sleep causing continuous power consumption

questions.question: What is Doze mode in Android? Explain its significance in battery optimization.
keyPoints: Doze mode restricts app activity when device is unplugged and stationary
Deferrs background activity and network access
Uses scheduled wake-ups for sync
Significantly extends battery life during periods of non-use
Apps must use Firebase Cloud Messaging or high-priority GCM for urgent notifications

questions.question: Describe the purpose and functionality of energy profiling tools in application development.
keyPoints: Measure power consumption of application components
Identify energy-intensive code sections and operations
Profile CPU, network, GPS, and sensor usage
Help developers make informed optimization decisions
Tools include Android Profiler, Battery Historian, Trepn Profiler

questions.question: Explain DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling) as a power management technique.
keyPoints: Adjusts processor voltage and frequency based on workload
Reduces power during low-demand periods
Power consumption is proportional to frequency and squared voltage
Common technique in mobile processors
Allows dynamic power management without hardware changes

questions.question: What is JobScheduler in Android and how does it contribute to energy efficiency?
keyPoints: API for scheduling deferrable background work
Allows apps to specify constraints (charging, idle, network)
Batches work to minimize device wake-ups
Defers non-critical tasks to optimal times
Reduces battery drain from scattered background activities

questions.question: Explain the concept of network batching for energy efficiency with an example.
keyPoints: Network batching groups multiple network requests together
Reduces the number of times the cellular radio is activated
Example: Instead of sending 100 separate requests, batch them into one request
Each radio activation consumes significant energy
Reduces overall energy consumption for network operations

questions.question: Discuss the various power management features in Android that help conserve battery life. Explain how each feature works and its impact on application behavior.
keyPoints: Doze mode - restricts activity when device is stationary and unplugged
App Standby - limits background activity for unused apps
Adaptive Battery - ML-based feature that learns usage patterns
Wakelocks - mechanism to keep device awake but drains battery
JobScheduler - defers work to optimal times (charging, idle)
App restrictions - system-imposed limits on background activity
Impact: Apps must be designed to work within these constraints

questions.question: Explain the energy consumption analysis process for mobile applications. Include discussion on profiling tools, measurement techniques, and optimization strategies.
keyPoints: Energy profiling tools - Android Profiler, Battery Historian, Trepn
Measurement techniques - hardware joulemeters, software profiling APIs
Analysis process - identify hotspots, measure component consumption
Optimization strategies - reduce network calls, batch operations, use efficient algorithms
Power states - active, idle, sleep states and their energy profiles
Code-level optimizations - efficient algorithms, caching, avoiding unnecessary work
Design considerations - architecture decisions affecting energy consumption

questions.question: Design an energy-efficient mobile application architecture. Discuss the principles, patterns, and best practices that should be followed to minimize energy consumption while maintaining good user experience.
keyPoints: Principle of minimal work - do only what's necessary
Batch processing - group network requests and computations
Lazy loading - defer expensive operations until needed
Efficient data structures and algorithms
Sensor management - release resources when not needed
Network optimization - use WiFi over cellular, compression, caching
Background processing - use JobScheduler for deferrable work
UI optimization - reduce screen-on time, efficient animations
Testing - use profiling tools throughout development
Trade-offs - balance between performance and energy consumption

questions.question: Explain the concept of power states in mobile devices and how applications interact with these states. Discuss how understanding power states helps in writing energy-efficient code.
keyPoints: Power states - active, idle, sleep, deep sleep
Active state - full power, CPU running at maximum
Idle state - reduced power, waiting for events
Sleep state - minimal power, RAM retained
Deep sleep - RAM in self-refresh, most components off
Application interactions - wake locks, alarms, background services
Energy implications of each state
Writing efficient code - minimize transitions to active state, use efficient algorithms, batch operations
